does: This paper applies mechanistic interpretability techniques to understand how Llava, a multimodal large language model, processes visual question answering tasks. The authors analyze VQA mechanisms by comparing them with textual QA on color-answering tasks, discovering that visual question answering operates through an in-context learning mechanism similar to text-only tasks. They demonstrate that visual features maintain high interpretability when projected into the language embedding space and show that visual instruction tuning enhances the underlying Vicuna language model's capabilities. The work culminates in developing an interpretability tool that identifies which visual regions most influence the model's predictions, with applications to understanding and potentially mitigating visual hallucination errors.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of mechanistic interpretability, which has primarily focused on understanding textual LLMs through activation analysis and circuit discovery, to the understudied domain of multimodal models. It builds on recent successes in interpreting transformer mechanisms and in-context learning in language models, while responding to the gap in understanding how vision and language components interact in MLLMs. The paper leverages established mechanistic interpretability methodologies—attention analysis, embedding space analysis, and feature visualization—but applies them to the novel challenge of explaining cross-modal reasoning. It connects to broader research on visual hallucinations in vision-language models and contributes to understanding how multimodal models integrate heterogeneous input modalities.
enables: This work establishes foundations for debugging and improving multimodal models through interpretability-driven analysis. By identifying which visual regions drive predictions, the approach enables targeted improvements to reduce visual hallucination, a critical failure mode in deployed MLLMs. The mechanistic understanding could facilitate development of more robust vision-language architectures by revealing design principles that preserve interpretability across modalities. Future work could extend these analysis tools to other multimodal tasks and datasets, develop interventions based on interpretability findings to mitigate hallucinations, or create safety mechanisms that leverage visibility into model reasoning. The framework also opens pathways for understanding how different fusion strategies between vision and language encoders affect the resulting learned mechanisms.
